source_paper_title: On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Dimou_2026, title={On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Dimou, Nikos}, year={2026}, month=mar }
context:
Section 7 (Future directions), page 34.

After proving that every two-player zero-sum game with cone-leveled strategy sets can be described by a primal-dual pair of conic linear programs, the following question naturally arises: When is a two-player zero-sum game equilibrium-equivalent to a game with cone-leveled strategy sets?
title: Equilibrium-equivalent reduction of zero-sum games to cone-leveled strategy games
background_latex:
A two-player zero-sum game is a triple \(G=(S,T,u)\), where \(S\subseteq X\) and \(T\subseteq Y\) are the strategy sets of players I and II, and \(u:S\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) is the payoff to player I.

In the setting considered here, \(X\) and \(Y\) are (reflexive) Banach spaces with continuous duals \(X^*\) and \(Y^*\). A bilinear pairing is denoted by \(\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle\). A (closed convex) cone \(C\subseteq X\) has positive dual cone \(C^*:=\{w\in X^*: \langle w,x\rangle\ge 0\ \forall x\in C\}\), and similarly for a cone \(K\subseteq Y\).

A cone-leveled set is a subset \(S\subseteq X\) that can be written as
\[
S=\{x\in C: \langle \alpha,x\rangle\in H\},
\]
for some convex cone \(C\subseteq X\), some nonzero functional \(\alpha\in X^*\), and some nonempty set \(H\subseteq (0,\infty)\) (typically an interval). A basic cone-leveled set is the special case \(H=\{p\}\) (a single level). A base of a cone is a basic cone-leveled set \(\{x\in C: \langle \alpha,x\rangle=1\}\) with \(\alpha\in \mathrm{int}(C^*)\).

Two games \(G=(S,T,u)\) and \(G'=(S',T',u')\) are equilibrium-equivalent if they have the same set of Nash equilibria after an identification of strategies (e.g., via maps between \(S\) and \(S'\), \(T\) and \(T'\)) that preserves saddle-point inequalities, so that equilibria and game values correspond under the identification.

The paper establishes that games whose strategy sets are cone-leveled (in particular, bases of convex cones) and whose payoff is bilinear \(u(x,y)=\langle y,Ax\rangle\) can be analyzed/solved via associated conic linear programs and duality. This motivates asking which more general games can be reduced, without changing equilibrium structure, to this cone-leveled class.
problem_definition_latex:
Characterize (or give verifiable necessary and sufficient conditions for) when a given two-player zero-sum game \(G=(S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent to a two-player zero-sum game \(\widetilde G=(\widetilde S,\widetilde T,\widetilde u)\) with cone-leveled strategy sets \(\widetilde S\subseteq \widetilde X\), \(\widetilde T\subseteq \widetilde Y\) and bilinear payoff of the form
\[
\widetilde u(\tilde x,\tilde y)=\langle \tilde y, \widetilde A\tilde x\rangle
\]
for some linear operator \(\widetilde A\), such that Nash equilibria (saddle points) of \(G\) correspond to Nash equilibria of \(\widetilde G\) under the equivalence mapping.

Characterize (or give verifiable necessary and sufficient conditions for) when a given two-player zero-sum game \(G=(S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent to a two-player zero-sum game \(\widetilde G=(\widetilde S,\widetilde T,\widetilde u)\) with cone-leveled strategy sets \(\widetilde S\subseteq \widetilde X\), \(\widetilde T\subseteq \widetilde Y\) and bilinear payoff of the form
\[
\widetilde u(\tilde x,\tilde y)=\langle \tilde y, \widetilde A\tilde x\rangle
\]
for some linear operator \(\widetilde A\), such that Nash equilibria (saddle points) of \(G\) correspond to Nash equilibria of \(\widetilde G\) under the equivalence mapping.
open_problem_quote: After proving that every two-player zero-sum game with cone-leveled strategy sets can be described by a primal-dual pair of conic linear programs, the following question naturally arises: When is a two-player zero-sum game equilibrium-equivalent to a game with cone-leveled strategy sets?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a general characterization of when an arbitrary game can be reduced (via equilibrium-preserving transformations) to the cone-leveled/bilinear class. There is no obvious finite-dimensional, efficiently checkable search space for counterexamples without first formalizing what class of equivalence mappings is allowed.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: If one fixes a concrete notion of equilibrium-equivalence (e.g., affine/linear embeddings of mixed-strategy extensions) and restricts to finite-dimensional games, an automated loop could search for embeddings into cone-leveled representations and score candidates by whether equilibria map correctly (computable via LP/SDP). However, in the general infinite-dimensional setting and without a specified equivalence notion, the search space and fitness signal are underspecified.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
No forward-citation analysis was provided, so the only citable item here is the source paper itself. The open problem asks for an equilibrium-preserving reduction of general two-player zero-sum games to the cone-leveled/bilinear class studied in the paper, where equilibria correspond to primal-dual solutions of conic programs.

Within the source framework, cone-leveled sets are intersections of cones with level sets of positive functionals, and bases of cones yield compactness in reflexive Banach spaces. The paper shows that for such games with bilinear payoff \(u(x,y)=\langle y,Ax\rangle\), minimax and Nash equilibria can be computed via conic linear programming duality, and it develops an “almost equivalence” between minimax and strong duality with a sharp fair-game pathology.

Because no subsequent literature is supplied, there is currently no evidence in the provided materials that the characterization problem has been solved. The problem therefore remains open based on the given data, and progress would likely require identifying invariants of equilibrium structure under strategy-space embeddings and positive-homogeneous reparametrizations that map general convex strategy sets into cone slices while preserving saddle-point inequalities.

sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces cone-leveled strategy sets in reflexive Banach spaces and proves that for bilinear-payoff games with such strategy sets, minimax and Nash equilibria can be obtained via associated conic linear programs; conversely, minimax implies strong duality except for a game-dependent fair-value pathology.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open question (Section 7) asking when an arbitrary zero-sum game is equilibrium-equivalent to one with cone-leveled strategy sets and bilinear payoff, motivating the desired characterization.
